content:
High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s foranion exchange membranes Triet Nguyen Dai Luong , Si Chen

, Patric Jannasch *

Centre for Analysis and Synthesis, Department of Chemistry, Lund University, SE-22100, Lund, Sweden

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Poly(arylene piperidinium)sAnion exchange membranesFused benzene ringsHydroxide conductivityAlkaline stability

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: A B S T R A C T Incorporating fused benzene rings in backbones of anion conducting polymers may significantly improve theproperties of anion exchange membranes (AEMs). Here, we have prepared and studied ether-free AEMs based onpoly(arylene piperidinium)s containing up to 50 % phenanthrene units. A series of terpolymers were prepared insuperacid-mediated polyhydroxyalkylations of phenanthrene, p-terphenyl and N-methylpiperidone. In order toefficiently vary the ion exchange capacity (IEC), a series of quaterpolymers was also prepared by adding 2,2,2trifluoroacetophenone. These precursor polymers were quaternized and cast into AEMs, which showed anincreasing level of phase separation and thermal stability with the phenanthrene content. The hydroxide conductivity of the AEMs was enhanced by the presence of phenanthrene and values up to 156 mS cm-1 at 80 ◦Cwere reached. Furthermore, the AEMs showed a high alkaline stability with, e.g., a mere 5 % ionic loss afterstorage in 2 M aq. NaOH for 15 days at 90 ◦C. The results demonstrate the positive effect of incorporatingphenanthrene units in the polymer backbone on the properties of AEMs, thus providing a successful strategydesign and develop AEMs containing fused benzene rings for alkaline electrochemical energy devices.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: 1. Introduction Anion exchange membranes (AEMs) are typically functionalizedwith quaternary ammonium cations and may thus selectively conductanions such as OH-or Cl-across the membrane. Consequently, AEMsare essential components in anion exchange membrane fuel cells(AEMFCs)andanionexchangemembranewaterelectrolyzers(AEMWEs), which haveattractedwidespread attentionand arecurrently being extensively developed [1–9]. These devices operateunder basic conditions and can thus utilize low-cost non-platinum groupmetal catalysts, thereby decreasing the cost of implementing theseemerging green hydrogen technologies [10,11]. Although significantprogress has been made, the widespread commercialization of AEMFCsand AEMWEs is currently hindered by lower performance and long-termstability compared to corresponding devices operating under acidicconditions using proton exchange membranes. To improve the performance of AEMFCs and AEMWEs, it is essential to develop AEMs thatcombine high hydroxide conductive with high chemical stability.Recent studies have shown that AEMs based on polymers containingfused benzene rings tend to form distinctly phase-separated morphologies, facilitating ion transport by generating continuous ion conducting water-rich channels. For instance, Xu et al. prepared AEMs containingpyrene, a compound consisting of four fused benzene rings, in thepolymer backbone [12]. They found that π-π stacking interactions between the pyrene units induced molecular assembly in the AEMs.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: benzene rings, in thepolymer backbone [12]. They found that π-π stacking interactions between the pyrene units induced molecular assembly in the AEMs. TheseAEMs reached fairly high OH-conductivity, 153 mS cm-1 at 90 ◦C, at alow IEC (2.03 mequiv g-1). However, if the pyrene content in the AEMswas too high, the AEMs became brittle due to the high rigidity of thepolymer backbones. Furthermore, the incorporation of anthracene,consisting of three "straight" fused benzene rings, into AEMs was reported by Liu et al. [13]. They reported that the π-π stacking interactionsof anthracene units facilitated the microphase separation and the formation of continuous ion conducting water-rich channels in the AEMs.Furthermore, it was reported that the presence of the rigid anthraceneunits in the polymer backbone increased the fraction of free volume,leading to improved ion conductivity. Hence, employing polymerbackbones containing fused benzene rings may significantly enhanceAEM properties, especially concerning morphology and conductivity.Poly(arylene piperidinium)s have emerged as attractive alkali-stableAEMs because of the absence of any ether links in the polymer backboneand the high stability of the N,N-dimethylpiperidinium (DMP) cations[6,14,15].Forexample,AEMsbasedonpoly(p-terphenyl

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: Available online 11 January 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). dimethylpiperidinium) showed a high alkaline stability with an ionicloss of only 5 % after storage in 2 M aq. NaOH at 90 ◦C during 15 days[16]. Moreover, these polymers are prepared via straightforwardsuperacid-mediated polyhydroxyalkylations with controlled ionic content and high molecular weights, ensuring excellent membrane properties [17–19]. In recent years, a large number of combinations ofdifferent arene monomers and piperidone have been employed to prepare poly(arylene piperidinium)s to tune and improve the properties ofAEMs used in AEMFCs [4,20–23], AEMWEs [24–26], and flow batteries[27,28]. Commercially available arenes for polyhydroxyalkylationsinclude diphenyl, bibenzyl, pand m-terphenyl, p,p-quaterphenyl and 9,9-dimethyl fluorene, and others have been prepared in-house, such as 9,9-dimethyl-2,7-diphenyl-9H-fluorene [29], 1,6-diphenylpyrene [12],and m,mand p,m-quaterphenyl [30].

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: and others have been prepared in-house, such as 9,9-dimethyl-2,7-diphenyl-9H-fluorene [29], 1,6-diphenylpyrene [12],and m,mand p,m-quaterphenyl [30]. The broad availability of differentarene monomers brings flexibility to the polymer structure design totune AEM properties, including the ionic content and the polymerbackbone stiffness which can be adjusted by the choice of monomers[31].In the present study, we have designed, prepared and investigated aseries of poly(arylene piperidinium) AEMs incorporating phenanthreneunits, which contain three "curved" fused aromatic rings. Two series ofterand quaterpolymers containing phenanthrene and piperidine unitswere first prepared in superacid-mediated polyhydroxyalkylations, followed by quaternization of the piperidine units to achieve the finalAEMs. In this way, AEMs with different contents of phenanthrene andpiperidinium were prepared to evaluate the effect of phenanthrene onmembrane performance. The AEMs were characterized with respect tomolecular structure, morphology, mechanical properties, water uptake,hydroxide conductivity, and thermal and alkaline stability. 2. Experimental 2.1.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: omolecular structure, morphology, mechanical properties, water uptake,hydroxide conductivity, and thermal and alkaline stability. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials Phenanthrene, 2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenone, trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TFSA, 99 %), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, 99 %), p-terphenyl (99%), iodomethane (98 % with stabilizer), N-methyl-4-piperidone (98 %),N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, reagent grade), dimethyl sulfoxide-d6(DMSO‑d6, 99.96 at% D), chloroform-d (CDCl3, 99.8 at% D), sodiumhydroxide (NaOH, ACS reagent, 97 %, pellet), potassium carbonate(K2CO3, ≥99 %), methyl iodide, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, reagentgrade) were purchase from Sigma Aldrich. Diethyl ether (ACS reagent grade), anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99.5 %) and anhydrous sodium bromide (NaBr, 99 %) were purchased from VWR. All chemicalswere used as received. Dichloromethane (DCM) for polymerization wascollected from an MBraun MB-SPS 800 dry solvent dispensing system. 2.2. Preparation of precursor terpolymers and quaterpolymers All the precursor polymers in this work were prepared in superacidmediated polyhydroxylalkylations, as seen in Scheme 1. The terpolymerprecursors were named as PPh-pre-x where x is the molar fractionphenanthrene-containing units in the polymers. The synthesis of thePPh-pre-0.5 precursor polymer is described below as an example.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: s the molar fractionphenanthrene-containing units in the polymers. The synthesis of thePPh-pre-0.5 precursor polymer is described below as an example. In a 25ml round-bottom flask, phenanthrene (0.267 g, 0.0015 mol, 1 equiv), pterphenyl (0.345 g, 0.0015 mol, 1 equiv), N-methyl-4-piperidone (0.443ml, 0.0036 mol, 2.4 equiv) were dissolved in 2.6 ml DCM. After themixture was cooled to 0 ◦C using an ice bath, TFA (0.23 ml, 0.003 mol, 2equiv) and TFSA (2.64 ml, 0.03 mol, 20 equiv) were consecutively addeddropwise. The reaction solution was then kept at 0 ◦C during the reaction. During this period, the viscosity of the reaction solution keptincreasing. After 5 h, the solution was poured into water to precipitatethe polymer as a white powder. The product was then filtered andwashed with water before being dried under vacuum at 50 ◦C to obtainthe final product.The precursor quaterpolymers, based on phenanthrene, p-terphenyl,N-methyl-4-piperidone, and 2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenone, were synthesized following the same procedure. The precursor quaterpolymers werenamed as PPhTAp-pre-iec, where iec is the IECOH–value calculated fromthe titration results. The ratio between N-methyl-4-piperidone and2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenone was adjusted to target final membraneswith ion exchange capacities (IECs) between 1.93 and 2.55 mequiv g-1.The reference poly(p-terphenyl piperidinium) sample was synthesized aspreviously reported [16]. 2.3.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: apacities (IECs) between 1.93 and 2.55 mequiv g-1.The reference poly(p-terphenyl piperidinium) sample was synthesized aspreviously reported [16]. 2.3. Quaternization The precursor polymers were quaternized by Menshutkin reactionswith methyl iodide using K2CO3 as a base to produce the final AEMpolymers. Here, the quaternization procedure of PPh-pre-0.5 is given asan example. PPh-pre-0.5 (1 g) and K2CO3 (1 g) were added to a 25 mlround bottom flask, followed by 10 ml NMP. Next, methyl iodide (1 ml)was added to the solution, and the flask was covered with aluminum foilto protect the methyl iodide. The reaction solution was stirred at room

content: Scheme 1. Synthetic pathways to precursor terand quaterpolymers and the corresponding piperidinium-functional phenanthrene polymers. T.N. Dai Luong et al.Journal of Membrane Science 719 (2025) 123724 temperature for 48 h and was then poured into diethyl ether to precipitate the polymer. After filtration and washes with DI water, the finalproduct was obtained as a yellow powder. 2.4. Membrane preparation AEMs were prepared by dissolving 0.15 g polymer in the iodide formin 3 ml NMP to achieve 5 wt% solutions. The solutions were passedthrough syringe filters (0.45 μm, VWR) onto Petri dishes (∅= 50 mm).The Petri dishes were placed in a ventilated casting oven at 80 ◦C for 48h. After casting, the membranes were peeled off from the Petri dishes,repeatedly washed with water, and ion exchanged by immersion in 1 Maq. NaBr for 78 h. Finally, the membranes were extensively washed withdeionized water before being stored in deionized water until furthercharacterization. 2.5. Characterization NMR spectroscopy. The structure of the polymers was characterized using NMR spectroscopy. 1H NMR data was collected with a BrukerDRX 400 spectrometer. Polymer samples were dissolved in DMSO‑d6 (δ= 2.50 ppm), before adding TFA (approximately 5 % volume) to shift thewater signal and protonate the amine in the alkaline stability test.Thermal characterization. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) wasemployed to study the thermal stability of the precursor polymers andAEMs.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: tability test.Thermal characterization. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) wasemployed to study the thermal stability of the precursor polymers andAEMs. The analyses were conducted on a TA Instruments TGA Q500under N2 atmosphere. Samples of the precursor polymers and AEMs inthe bromide form were preheated to 110 ◦C for 20 min. The sampleswere then cooled to 50 ◦C and heated to 600 ◦C at a rate of 10 ◦C per min.Data was recorded during the heating process from 50 to 600 ◦C, and thedecomposition temperature (Td,95) was taken at the point of 5 % weightloss.Intrinsic viscosity. The intrinsic viscosity ([η]) of the polymers wasmeasured at 25 ◦C by using an Ubbelohde viscometer. Polymer sampleswere dried at 50 ◦C for 2 days and weighed before dissolution of around0.1 g polymer in 10 ml solution of 0.1 M LiBr in DMSO. The polymerconcentration (c) of the solution was then successively reduced byadding 1, 2, and 2 ml, respectively, of 0.1 M LiBr in DMSO to obtainthree additional concentrations. The efflux time of the blank solution(0.1 M LiBr in DMSO) and the different sample solutions through thecapillary was recorded four times for each solution. The reduced (ηred)and inherent (ηinh) viscosities were calculated as: ηred = tsample tblank -1 c(1) ηinh = ln( tsample tblank ) c(2) where tsample and tblank are efflux time of the sample solution and cthe concentration of the polymer solution.

content: c(1) ηinh = ln( tsample tblank ) c(2) where tsample and tblank are efflux time of the sample solution and cthe concentration of the polymer solution. The [η] values were thendetermined by the intercept at the y-axis after extrapolating ηinh and ηredto c = 0.Ion exchange capacity. The ion exchange capacity (IEC) of theAEMs was determined by Mohr titrations. Approx. 30 mg of each AEMsample in the bromide form was dried under vacuum for 48 h at 50 ◦C.The samples were weighed before immersion in 25 ml of 0.2 M aq.NaNO3 at 40 ◦C for 72 h to accomplish complete ion exchange. Eachsample was titrated four times with 0.01 aq. AgNO3 by extracting 5 mlportions of the solutions, using 0.1 M aq. K2CrO4 as color indicator. Thereported IECBr - values were obtained as the average of the four titrations. The IEC in the hydroxide form was calculated as: IECOH - =IECBr1-0.0629 * IECBr-(3) Water uptake. AEM samples in the bromide form were dried under vacuum and weighed to obtain mdry,Br-. The samples were thenimmersed in 1 M aq. NaOH for 48 h to ion exchanged to the hydroxideform. Subsequently, the samples were quickly washed and stored in DIwater at 20 ◦C. After 24 h, the samples were taken out, weighed to obtainmwet,OH-. For measurements at 40, 60, and 80 ◦C, the samples werestored in DI water for 8 h after exchange to OH-form before measuringthe weight.

The water uptake was calculated as: WU = mwet,OH- - mdry,OHmdry,OH-(4) where mdry,OHwas calculated from the IECBr-values as: mdry,OH -= mdry,Br -* (1 -0.0629 * IECBr -)(5) Swelling ratio. The dimensional changes in length (l) and thickness(t) were measured alongside the water uptake in order to determine thein-plane (SWi) and through-plane (SWth) swelling at 20, 40, 60, and80 ◦C. the swelling ratios were then calculated as follows: SWi = lwet,OH-- ldry,Brldry,Br-(6) SWth = twet,OH-- tdry,Brtdry,Br-(7) Ion conductivity. The hydroxide conductivity of the AEMs at 20, 40,60, and 80 ◦C was measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS). AEM samples in the bromide form (1.4 * 1.4 cm) were preparedand immersed in 1 M aq. NaOH during 2 days at room temperature. TheNaOH solution was replaced three times during the immersion. Thesamples were then washed thoroughly with degassed DI water andstored in degassed DI water overnight in a desiccator with nitrogenpurge. The measurements were conducted after placing the samples in asealed 2-probe cell in a Novocontrol high-resolution dielectric analyzerV 1.01S operating at 10 mV and a frequency between 100 to 107 Hz. Theoriginal thickness of the samples before the measurements were used tocalculate the conductivity values.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

Moreover, measurements were carriedout on three samples per reported conductivity value, and the averagevalues were reported together with the population standard deviation.Small angle X-ray scattering. To study the bulk morphology of theAEMs, small angle X-ray scattering of the dry AEMs in the bromide formwere measured with a SAXSLAB SAXS instrument (JJ X-ray Systems Aps,Denmark) equipped with a Pilatus detector. The radiation was Cu Kαwith wavelength (l) of 1.542 Å. The wave vector (q) was determined as: q =4πl sin 2θ(8) where 2θ is the scattering angle. The separation length (d) wasdetermined by the Bragg’s law: d = 2π q(9) Atomic Force Microscopy. A Bruker Icon Atomic Force Microscopeinstrument with AC240TS-R3 tips was used to record the phase imagesof dry AEM samples in bromide form under the tapping mode.Mechanical properties. Mechanical properties of the AEMs wereevaluated by studying the stress-strain properties of dry AEM samples inthe bromide form using a TA Instruments Q800dynamic mechanicalanalysis (DMA). Rectangular samples (approx. 0.5 cm * 3 cm, 50–60 μmin thickness) were prepared and mounted between the tension clampsbefore applying a 0.05 N preload force. The measurements were thenconducted with a ramping force of 0.5 N min-1 at 30 ◦C in a controlledforce mode.

The deviation of the 0.5 %secant modulus measured in the wet state was not reported, because thevalues were below 1.Alkaline stability. AEM samples were placed in sealed pressureresistant tubes filled with 2 and 5 M aq. NaOH solutions, respectively.The tubes were then stored at 90 ◦C over 15 days. After this period, thesamples were taken out and converted to the bromide form by immersion in 1 M aq. NaBr solution at room temperature for 24 h. Next, thesamples were washed with DI water and dried under vacuum. Anychanges in the polymer structure were monitored by 1H NMR spectroscopy of AEMs dissolved in DMSO‑d6 containing 5–10 vol% TFA.Spectra of samples before and after immersion in alkaline solution werecompared to determine degradation mechanisms and the extent of ionicloss. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Polymer synthesis and characterization The precursor N-methylpiperidine-containing polymers were synthesized by superacid-mediated polyhydroxyalkylations, as depicted inScheme 1. First, phenanthrene and p-terphenyl monomers were polymerized with N-methyl-4-piperidone in a mixture of TFSA, TFA andDMC at 0 ◦C to form a series of precursor terpolymers (PPh-pre-x).

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: l monomers were polymerized with N-methyl-4-piperidone in a mixture of TFSA, TFA andDMC at 0 ◦C to form a series of precursor terpolymers (PPh-pre-x). Themolar phenanthrene to p-terphenyl ratio was set to 0.25:0.75, 0.37:0.63and 0.5:0.5 to synthesize PPh-pre-0.25, PPh-pre-0.37, and PPh-pre-0.50,respectively. When molar fractions of phenanthrene above 0.5 were used, the synthesized polymers showed poor ability to form films,possibly a consequence of the rigid structure of phenanthrene. Tofacilitate the polymerization, the piperidone was used in a 20 mol%excess. The viscosity of the polymerization solutions increased quickly,which indicated a high reactivity of phenanthrene with the N-methyl-4piperidone monomer. The molecular weights of phenanthrene and pterphenyl are quite close (178 vs. 230 g mol-1), leading to a narrow IECrange (2.85–2.92 mequiv g-1) of the AEMs derived from the terpolymersin the PPh-pre-x series. Hence, in order to expand the IEC span, we alsoprepared a series of quaterpolymers (denoted PPhTAp-pre-iec, where iecis the IEC of the final AEMs in the hydroxide form) by including 2,2,2trifluoroacetophenone in the polymerizations (Scheme 1). The molarratios between 2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenone and N-methyl-4-piperidonewere regulated to obtain AEMs with the desired IEC, while keeping themolar phenanthrene:p-terphenyl ratio fixed at 50:50.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

However, toinvestigate the effect of the presence of phenanthrene on the overallAEM properties, two quaterpolymers with approximately the samepiperidine content, but different phenanthrene contents were also synthesized (i.e., PPhTAp-pre-2.14 with IEC = 2.14 mequiv g-1 and aphenanthrene:p-terphenyl molar ratio of 50:50, and PPhTAp-pre-2.180.25 with IEC = 2.18 mequiv g-1 and a phenanthrene:p-terphenyl molarratio of 25:75). All the piperidine-functional precursor polymers weresubsequently quaternized in Menshutkin reactions with methyl iodide.The quaternizations were performed in NMP at room temperature for 48h to achieve the final AEM terpolymers named PPh-x, where x is themolar fraction phenanthrene-containing units in the AEM polymers, andthe final AEM quaterpolymers PPhTAp-iec, where iec is IECOH–of themembranes in the hydroxide form calculated from titration data.The structure of the precursor polymers and the quaternized polymers were verified by 1H NMR data (Fig. 1 and Figs. S1–S10) recordedusing DMSO‑d6 after the addition of TFA. In the spectra of the precursor

Furthermore,distinct signals originating from protonated tertiary piperidine groups(N+-H) were seen between 9 and 10 ppm in the same spectra. After thequaternization of the precursor polymers with methyl iodide to obtainthe N,N-dimethylpiperidinium (DMP) cations of the final AEM polymers, the 1H signals characteristically broadened. The complete quaternization was verified by the complete absence of any signalsemerging from protonated tertiary piperidine (+N–H, Fig. 1a and b).The molecular weight is one of the most important polymer characteristics determining the properties of AEMs.

Thevalues ranged from [ƞ] = 0.34 to 0.52 dL g-1 (Table 1 and Fig. S13),which indicated that medium to high molecular weight polymers wereproduced in the polymerzations. These intrinsic viscosity values alsodemonstrated the high reactivity of the phenanthrene monomer in thepolyhydroxyalkylations.

content:
3.2. Membrane preparation and morphology Transparent, mechanically robust and flexible AEMs from the PPh-xand PPhTAp-iec series were cast from DMSO solutions at 80 ◦C. Themembranes were ion exchanged to the bromide form and stored indeionized water until further characterizations. The IECBr-values of theAEMs determined by Mohr titrations (Table 1) were in excellentagreement with the expected values calculated from the monomer feedratios. This demonstrated an efficient control of the polymer composition and IEC by the monomer ratios used in the polymerizations.Small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) was used to study the bulkmorphology of the dry AEMs in the bromide form. The SAXS profiles didnot reveal any distinct ionomer peaks, although a weak and broad peakappearing at q = 0.32 Å-1 (corresponding to a characteristic distance ofd = 1.96 nm) was noted in the profile of membrane PPh-0.25 (Fig. S11).This may be explained by the high rigidity of the phenanthrenecontaining polymer backbones and the limited local mobility of thebackbone-tethered DMP cations during the membrane casting.To further study the morphology of the AEMs, atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed to investigate surfaces of dry AEM samplesin the bromide form. In the PPh-x series, PPh-0.25 and PPh-0.50

Fig. 2. AFM phase images of a) a reference poly(p-terphenyl piperidinium) AEM, b) PPh-0.25, c) PPh-0.50, d) PPhTAp-2.18-0.25 and e) PPhTAp-2.14.

content: Fig. 3. Stress-strain data of the PPhTAp-iec membranes in the bromide form recorded in the a) dry and b) wet state at 30 ◦C under a 0.5 N min-1 ramping force test. T.N. Dai Luong et al.Journal of Membrane Science 719 (2025) 123724 membranes were selected and compared with a reference poly(p-terphenyl piperidinium) AEM. Fig. 2a–c clearly shows that an increase ofthe phenanthrene content, from 0 % in poly(p-terphenyl piperidinium)to 25 % in PPh-0.25, and 50 % in PPh-0.50, promoted the phase separation of hydrophilic (dark regions) and hydrophobic (bright regions)domains. Membrane PPh-0.50 showed the most ordered and regularmorphology. To continue investigating the effect of the phenanthrenecontent in the PPhTAp-iec series, two membranes with almost the sameIEC, but different phenanthrene content were studied, i.e., PPhTAp2.18-0.25 (IECOH-= 2.18, 25 % phenanthrene) and PPhTAp-2.14(IECOH-= 2.14, 50 % phenanthrene). As seen in Fig. 2e, membranePPhTAp-2.14 showed a more organized and regular morphology thanPPhTAp-2.18-0.25 (Fig. 2d). Hence, the AFM study showed that an increase of the phenanthrene content promoted the formation of a phaseseparation morphology in the AEMs.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: 3.3. Mechanical properties The AEMs in PPhTAp-iec series were selected for mechanical testingbecause of their restricted swelling and water uptake (discussed insection 3.4), which make them more attractive for practical applications. Stress-strain data, including the 0.5 % secant modulus, of theAEMs in the dry and wet state are reported in Fig. 3 and Table 2. ThePPhTAp-iec AEMs showed excellent tensile strength. All the AEMs inPPhTAp-iec series had a tensile strength above 35 MPa in the dry state.The 0.5 % secant modulus values of PPhTAp-iec AEMs in the range1.8–2.1 GPa (Table 2). Throughout the mechanical evaluation, a strongconnection between the mechanical properties and molecular weight ofthe AEM polymers was observed. For example, PPhTAp-2.18-0.25membrane had the highest intrinsic viscosity in the series with [ƞ] =0.52 showed the best mechanical properties, while the lowest mechanical strength was noted for PPhTAp-2.14, which membrane had thelowest intrinsic viscosity ([ƞ] = 0.34). As expected, in the wet state therewas a decrease in the tensile strength and an increase in the strain atbreak of the PPhTAp-iec AEMs in comparison with data recorded in drystate. The tensile strength of the wet PPhTAp-iec AEMs ranged from 13to 20 MPa, while the strain at break varied between 11.6 and 20.9 %. Inthe wet state, PPhTAp-2.14 had the highest strain at break and thesecond-highest tensile strength, which was in contrast to data in the dry state.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: . Inthe wet state, PPhTAp-2.14 had the highest strain at break and thesecond-highest tensile strength, which was in contrast to data in the dry state. This possibly indicated that the level of the water uptake significantly dictated the mechanical behavior of the PPhTAp-iec AEMs in thewet state, while the mechanical properties mainly depended on themolecular weight in the dry state. Consequently, the mechanicalstrength and modulus of the PPhTAp-iec AEMs indicated that they aresuitable for use in electrochemical devices.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: 3.4. Water uptake and swelling ratio The water in AEMs promotes ion dissociation and facilitates the ionconduction across the membrane [32]. However, excessive water uptakereduces the mechanical strength of the membrane and eventually alsothe conductivity [33]. The water uptake and swelling data of the currentAEMs are seen in Table 1 and Fig. 4. As expected, the water uptake of theAEMs increased with the temperature and IEC. The AEMs in the PPh-xseries, which had very high IECOHvalues, ranging from 2.85 to 2.92,showed excessive water uptake and swelling. The water uptake ofPPh-0.25 was 135 % already at 20 ◦C, and for PPh-0.50, it was close to200 % at the same temperature. When the temperature exceeded 40 ◦C,the PPh-x membranes kept swelling excessively and turned slightlysticky. Hence, because of the high IEC and water uptake, these fullyhydrated AEMs were difficult to handle at temperatures approaching80 ◦C. Thus, the water uptake could only be reliably measured at 20 and40 ◦C for these membranes. This behavior makes the PPh-x series unsuitable for use as AEMs with pure water. However, these membranesmay still be useful for AEMWEs operating with an aq. KOH supportelectrolyte, since the electrolyte uptake usually is substantially lowerthan the uptake of pure water [34]. For instance, the uptake of 1 M and2 M aq. KOH solution by membrane PPh-0.50 was 114 and 90 %,respectively (Table S1). These values were significantly lower than theuptake of pure water (198 %).

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: OH solution by membrane PPh-0.50 was 114 and 90 %,respectively (Table S1). These values were significantly lower than theuptake of pure water (198 %). Because of their high water uptake, thepolymers in the PPh-x series may also be useful as ionomers in AEMFCcathodes [5].In the PPhTAp-iec series, the nonionic 2,2,2-trifluoroacetophenonemonomer was applied to adjust and reduce the IEC of the AEMs.Hence, the water uptake of the AEMs in the PPhTAp-iec series wasreduced and more controlled. As seen in Fig. 4, the water uptake rangedfrom 61 % for PPhTAp-1.93 to 206 % for PPhTAp-2.55 at 80 ◦C. Thesevalues were significantly lower than observed for the PPh-x series,

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: 3.5. Hydroxide conductivity Several factors influence the ion conductivity of AEMs, including theIEC, water uptake, temperature, and morphology. In the present study,the hydroxide conductivity was measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) between 20 and 80 ◦C with the AEMs fullyimmersed in water. As seen in Fig. 5a, the conductivity of the AEMs inboth the PPh-x and PPhTAp-iec series increased with the IEC and wateruptake of the AEMs, as expected. An increase of the phenanthrenecontent in the PPh-x series led to a slight increase in the IEC values andthe water uptake because of the lower molecular weight of phenanthrene compared to p-terphenyl. When comparing the conductivity between the two series, the PPh-x AEMs showed a significantly lowerhydroxide conductivity in comparison with the AEMs in the PPhTAp-iecseries, despite their higher IECs. For example, at 80 ◦C the conductivityof PPh-0.50 (IECOH- = 2.92 mequiv g-1) was 128 mS cm-1, whilePPhTAp-2.55 with a lower IEC reached a conductivity of 156 mS cm-1.The reason can most probably be attributed to the excessive water uptake of the AEMs in PPh-x series, which caused a dilution of the chargecarrier (OH-) concentration [35]. Among the AEMs in the PPhTAp-iecseries, PPhTAp-2.55 with the highest IEC showed the highest conductivity (156 mS cm-1), but also the highest water uptake and swelling because of the high IEC.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: PhTAp-2.55 with the highest IEC showed the highest conductivity (156 mS cm-1), but also the highest water uptake and swelling because of the high IEC. To further clarify the correlation between theconductivity and water content of the AEMs in the PPhTAp-iec series, theconductivity was plotted as a function of the hydration number (λ)(Fig. 5b). The results showed that the conductivity increased moresteeply with λ for AEMs with low IECs (i.e., PPhTAp-1.93, PPhTAp-2.14,and PPhTAp-2.18-0.25) compared with AEMs having high IECs (i.e.,PPhTAp-2.39 and PPhTAp-2.55), indicating that the water was usedmore efficiently for hydroxide conduction in the former AEMs. In acomparison of PPhTAp-2.18-0.25 and PPhTAp-2.14, containing 25 and50 % phenanthrene, respectively, but having nearly the same IEC, thelatter AEM displayed a much steeper increase in conductivity with λcompared with the former AEM. The resulting ion conductivity ofPPhTAp-2.14was144mScm-1,whichsurpassedthatofPPhTAp-2.18-0.25 (122 mS cm-1) at 80 ◦C. This may indicate a positiveeffect of phenanthrene monomer on the performance of the AEMs. Wehypothesized that the presence of the fused-ring phenanthrene unitsfacilitated phase separation and ionic clustering in the AEMs, which wasalso indicated by the AFM results.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

3.6. Alkaline stability The strongly basic environment in AEMFCs and AEMWEs requiresAEMs with a high alkaline stability. In the present study, three representative AEMs with the same phenanthrene content, but with differentIECs and water uptake, were selected for evaluation of the alkalinestability.Hence,PPh-0.5,PPThAp-2.14,andPPhTAp-2.55wereimmersed in 2 and 5 M aq. NaOH solutions, respectively, at 90 ◦C during15 days. After this treatment, the AEMs were ion exchanged to the

content: Scheme 2. Common degradation pathways and products of the DMP cation formed by OH-attack and protonation by TFA, including (1) ring opening Hofmannβ-elimination, (2) ring opening substitution, and methyl substitution (3). T.N. Dai Luong et al.Journal of Membrane Science 719 (2025) 123724 bromide form and dissolved in DMSO‑d6 with the addition of 5 % TFAfor 1H NMR analysis. TFA was added to shift the water signal and toprotonate any tertiary amine groups formed by the degradation of DMPcations. Any new signals appearing after the alkaline treatment areuseful to elucidate the degradation pathway of the DMP cations. Inaddition, the extent of the ionic loss can be calculated from the corresponding signal integrals.The degradation mechanisms of DMP cation under aqueous alkalineconditions have been extensively studied and reported [16,35–37].Under harsh alkaline conditions, the DMP cation may undergo ring-opening degradation, either by Hofmann elimination at β-hydrogens, or through nucleophilic substitution at α-carbons. Methyl substitution is a further degradation pathway. These degradation pathwayslead to cationic loss and the formation of tertiary amines, which maysubsequently be protonated by TFA in the NMR solution, as displayed inScheme 2.

citation.title: High-performing phenanthrene-containing poly(arylene piperidinium)s for anion exchange membranes

content: yslead to cationic loss and the formation of tertiary amines, which maysubsequently be protonated by TFA in the NMR solution, as displayed inScheme 2. Depending on the harshness of the alkaline conditions, thenature of the polymer backbone, and the position of the DMP cations inthe polymer structure, one or two degradation pathways are usuallymore dominating than the others.The 1H NMR spectra recorded to monitor the ionic loss and degradation of the AEMs are collected in Fig. 6. After 15 days in 2 M aq. NaOHat 90 ◦C, the results only indicated barely detectable signs of AEMdegradation. To accelerate the degradation, fresh AEM samples werestored in 5 M aq. NaOH at 90 ◦C over 15 days. After this treatment, thespectra of all the samples revealed new signals appearing at 5.0, 5.3, and6.5 ppm, respectively (Fig. 6). These vinylic signals indicated ionic lossvia the Hofmann ring-opening β-elimination reaction. Additional signalsappeared at around 2.5 ppm to indicate further groups formed by theionic loss, possibly resulting from the methyl groups of tertiary amines.Moreover, the signal at 9.8 ppm originated from protonated tertiaryamine groups formed during the ionic loss, and was utilized to calculatethe total ionic loss of the AEMs after the alkaline treatment. From thevalues shown in Table 3, Hofmann elimination was clearly the majorcause of the ionic loss for all AEMs. The total ionic loss of the PPh-0.50membrane after 15 days at 90 ◦C in 2 M aq.

content: elimination was clearly the majorcause of the ionic loss for all AEMs. The total ionic loss of the PPh-0.50membrane after 15 days at 90 ◦C in 2 M aq. NaOH was very similar topreviously reported data of poly(terphenyl piperidinium) AEMs underthe same condition [16]. When comparing the alkaline stability of theAEMs, PPh-0.50 was the most stable and PPhTAp-2.55 had a slightlyhigher stability than PPhTAp-2.14. The most probable reason was thatPPh-0.50 had the highest water uptake. An increased λ value is known todecrease the nucleophilicity and reactivity of the hydroxide ions [38].Hence, the DMP cations of PPh-0.50 degraded the slowest, followed byPPhTAp-2.55 and PPhTAp-2.14. 3.7. Thermal stability The thermal stability of the precursor polymers and the AEMs in thebromide form was evaluated by TGA, and the resulting thermaldecomposition temperatures (Td,95) of the AEMs are reported in Table 1.All the precursors and AEM samples showed a high thermal stability,and almost all samples revealed Td,95 values above 300 ◦C (Fig. 7 andS12). This is sufficient for application in AEMFCs and AEMWEs, wherethe operating temperature typically ranges from 60 to approximately100 ◦C. As expected, the AEMs showed a lower thermal stability than thecorresponding precursor polymers because of the thermal sensitivity ofthe DMP cations in relation to the N-methylpiperidine groups. Analysisof the data in the PPh-x series showed that Td,95 increased withincreasing phenanthrene content.

content: 0.50 contained 25, 37, and 50 mol% phenanthrene units, respectively,and displayed Td,95 values of 313, 323, and 338 ◦C, respectively. Thesame trend was observed for the corresponding precursor polymers inthe PPh-pre-x series (Fig. S12). The degradation of the AEMs started withthe decomposition of the DMP cations at around 350 ◦C, followed by thedecomposition of the polymer backbone at around 500 ◦C. 4. Conclusions We have successfully prepared two series of AEMs with variouscontents of the fused benzene ring monomer phenanthrene in thepolymer backbones. The precursor terand quaterpolymers were synthesized by polyhydroxyalkylations, resulting in moderate to high molecular weight ether-free polymers. The phenanthrene content, up to 50%, resulted in improved overall properties of the AEMs. A high phenanthrene content clearly promoted the formation of a phase separatedmorphology, as well as the ion conductivity of the AEMs. Notably, anAEM containing a higher content of phenanthrene reached higher conductivities compared to an AEM with higher IEC and lower phenanthrene content. The current AEMs not only possessed high ionconductivity, but also had a high alkaline stability because of thechemical stability of the DMP cation. The results of the present studyhave demonstrated the positive effect of incorporating phenanthreneunits in the polymer backbone on the performance of AEMs, thusproviding a successful strategy design and develop AEMs incorporatingfused benzene rings.
